senses.definition: Capable of being absorbed or assimilated.
examples: The new material is sorbable and can trap moisture from the air.
Only sorbable compounds should be used in this experiment.

senses.definition: A genus of flowering plants in the family Platanaceae; note: rare or specialized usage.
examples: The botanical text mentions sorbaria as a less common ornamental shrub.
Identifying sorbaria requires expert examination.

senses.definition: Plural of sorbaria; a genus of shrubs in the stonecrop family (more commonly sorbaria is a genus name).
examples: The garden features several sorbarias.
Botanists discussed sorbarias during the field trip.

senses.definition: A salt or ester of sorbic acid used as a preservative.
examples: Propionic and sorbate preservatives help extend shelf life.
Sorbate is added to foods to prevent mold growth.

senses.definition: A salt or ester of sorbic acid used to preserve products.
examples: Potassium sorbate is a common sorbate in snacks.
Natural sorbate alternatives are sometimes preferred.

senses.definition: Plural of sorbate; preservative salts or esters of sorbic acid.
examples: Various sorbates are used to keep foods fresh.
Sorbates can inhibit fungal growth in products.

senses.definition: Past tense of sorb; absorbed or taken in.
examples: The sponge sorbed all the spilled juice.
Moisture sorbed into the fabric after the rain.

senses.definition: A substance that promotes or enhances the absorption of another substance.
examples: The sorbefacient properties of certain solvents can increase drug uptake.
Researchers studied sorbefacients to improve delivery systems.

senses.definition: Having the property of promoting absorption.
examples: A sorbefacient additive was included in the formulation.
The sorbefacient compound increases transfer across membranes.

senses.definition: Plural of sorbefacient; substances that promote absorption.
examples: Various sorbefacients were tested for effectiveness.
The study compared different sorbefacients in the delivery system.

senses.definition: A material that absorbs liquids or gases; used in filters and dehumidifiers.
examples: Activated charcoal is a powerful sorbent.
The spill was contained by a sorbent pad.

senses.definition: Plural of sorbent; substances that absorb liquids or gases.
examples: Different sorbents are used for oils and for water.
Industrial sorbents help clean up chemical spills.

senses.definition: A frozen dessert made from fruit juice or purée, sugar, and water.
examples: I ordered lemon sorbet for a refreshing dessert.
Sorbet is usually lighter than ice cream.

senses.definition: Plural of sorbet; frozen fruit desserts.
examples: The menu lists several sorbets, including mango and lime.
We shared two sorbets after the main course.

senses.definition: Relating to Sorbs, a Slavic ethnic group in eastern Germany; also a language name.
examples: Sorbian languages are part of the Slavic family.
Sorbian culture has its own traditions.

senses.definition: Relating to sorbic acid or its salts; used in chemistry and food science.
examples: Sorbic acid and its salts are used as preservatives.
Sorbic compounds prevent mold growth.

senses.definition: Capable of being absorbed; absorbable.
examples: The sorbile layer traps moisture effectively.
Not all materials are sorble; some resist absorption.

senses.definition: A chemical compound; specific context-dependent meaning (often historical or niche).
examples: The synthesis used a sorbin intermediate.
Researchers referenced sorbin in older literature.

senses.definition: Present participle of sorb; absorbing or taking in.
examples: The sponge is sorbing water quickly.
The material sorbing properties are strong.

senses.definition: A sugar derivative related to sorbinose used in chemistry or biochemistry.
examples: Sorbinose derivatives were studied in the lab.
The synthesis pathway included sorbinose.

senses.definition: Relating to,sorbic; not widely used; may appear in niche literature.
examples: The sorbish properties were noted in early studies.

senses.definition: A class of sugar alcohol derivatives used as emulsifiers in foods and cosmetics.
examples: Sorbitan esters act as surfactants in creams.
Sorbitan is common in ready-to-use frosting formulations.

senses.definition: Plural of sorbitan; sugar alcohol derivatives used as emulsifiers.
examples: Different sorbitans provide various emulsifying properties.
Sorbitans are widely used in cosmetics.

senses.definition: A mineral or mineral-like substance; context-dependent term.
examples: The geologist found a sample of sorbite.
Sorbite was described in the mineral catalog.

senses.definition: Plural of sorbite; mineral samples or related substances.
examples: Several sorbites were displayed in the cabinet.
Laboratory notes mention sorbites from the quarry.

senses.definition: Relating to sorbitol or sorbitol derivatives; organic chemistry term.
examples: Sorbitic compounds are studied for their sweetening properties.
The sorbitic pathway was described in the review.

senses.definition: The process of converting or forming into sorbitan/sorbitic derivatives; very specialized term.
examples: Sorbitisation is discussed in advanced organic chemistry texts.
The reactor enables rapid sorbitisation of substrates.
